A research group adopts the proposal anyway. Each round, generated abstractions are evaluated, and the accepted ones are returned to the draw pool so later rounds can build from them. Because accepted abstractions alter what is likely to be drawn next, the search is path dependent by construction.

Now suppose the first accepted abstractions are only mediocre. They need not be false; they only need to be good enough under the current evaluator to pass. Once returned to the pool, they become common ingredients in later candidates. Later search is then disproportionately organized around them, not because they are best, but because they arrived early and are available. As a result, future directions reflect historical accident.

The proposal also relies on recursive self-improvement by self-judgment. For that to work, the evaluator must be more stable than the generator. Otherwise, each generation of outputs is judged by a moving standard no firmer than the thing being improved. But the stability of the evaluation function is not explained. So there are two possibilities.

If the evaluator is unstable, recursive self-improvement has no clear anchor. The system may appear to improve relative to its latest tastes while losing any consistent notion of quality across rounds. Combined with path dependence, this means early mediocre abstractions can become entrenched simply because successive evaluators continue to inherit or amplify the same local preferences.

If the evaluator is stable, then its stability must come from somewhere outside the generator’s fluctuations. But that source is unspecified. Without it, the claim of plausible self-improvement remains unsupported rather than refuted; the mechanism is missing.

Bias is treated as a detail to work out, but in this setting bias is deeper than social or political skew. It includes ontological bias toward familiar causal grammars: the system more readily represents and rewards explanations that fit the kinds of causal structures it already recognizes. In the draw-pool scheme, this bias compounds. Familiar grammars produce acceptable abstractions earlier; early acceptance makes them more available; greater availability makes future generations even more likely to use them; repeated reuse makes the evaluator see them as normal, coherent, or promising. So the system does not merely prefer some answers. It preferentially expands one style of world-modeling.

The two nonsensical-seeming strings are premises too. Therefore they exist in the proposal’s discursive environment as tokens without provided interpretation. A stable evaluator would need some way to handle such uninterpreted material consistently: reject it, quarantine it, or map it to something meaningful. An unstable evaluator might treat it inconsistently across rounds, further evidencing drift. If such strings entered the accepted pool by mistake, path dependence would let even arbitrary artifacts shape future search simply by being early and reusable.

So the collision of premises implies a system whose outputs are highly sensitive to initial acceptances, whose claimed recursive improvement lacks an explained fixed standard, and whose biases operate at the level of what kinds of causality can even be discovered. In that world, compounding discoveries back into the pool risks locking search into historically contingent, ontologically narrow trajectories.
